exercises.title: The non-stationarity problem
exercises.prompt: Explain why putting two learning agents in the same environment breaks the single-agent RL assumptions. What is the non-stationarity problem, and why does it violate the Markov property that Q-learning's convergence relies on?
exercises.hint: Each agent is part of the others' environment; as others learn, the environment changes under your feet. Next state depends on others' (moving) policies → Markov assumption broken → no stationary target → convergence proofs fail.
exercises.solution:
**Why the single-agent assumptions break:** single-agent RL assumes a **stationary environment** — the transition dynamics `P(s'|s,a)` don't change over time, so there's a fixed optimal policy to converge to. With **multiple learning agents**, from the perspective of any one agent, **the other agents are part of its environment**. As those other agents **learn and change their policies**, the effective dynamics that agent faces **change too** — the environment is **non-stationary**.

**The non-stationarity problem:** the next state (and reward) depends not just on your action but on **what the other agents chose**, and their policies are **moving targets** that shift every time they update. So the same `(s, a)` can lead to different outcome distributions at different times in training — the 'rules' keep changing underneath you.

**Why it violates the Markov property:** the Markov assumption is 'the next state depends only on the **current state and my action**', i.e. `P(s'|s,a)` is well-defined and fixed. In a multi-agent setting `s'` also depends on the **other agents' actions, drawn from their *current, changing* policies** — which are not part of your state. So `P(s'|s,a)` is **not stationary** (it depends on hidden, time-varying policy parameters of others), and the clean Markov MDP no longer holds.
**Consequences:** 
- **Tabular convergence proofs fail** — Q-learning's guarantee assumes a stationary MDP; with a moving target there's no fixed `Q*` to converge to.
- **Naive deep RL also breaks** — agents **chase each other in cycles** (you adapt to their policy, they adapt to yours, round and round) and never settle into a stable joint policy; learning can oscillate or collapse.
This is why multi-agent RL needs **special techniques** (centralized training, self-play, league play) rather than just running independent single-agent learners.

exercises.title: Centralized training, decentralized execution (CTDE)
exercises.prompt: Explain the CTDE paradigm and why it tames non-stationarity. Describe how a centralized critic (as in MADDPG) uses global information during training while each agent acts on local observations at execution, and what a counterfactual baseline (COMA) adds for credit assignment.
exercises.hint: Train with access to all agents' obs/actions (critic sees everything → stationary from its view); execute with only local policy. Centralized critic conditions on joint actions. COMA: counterfactual baseline isolates one agent's contribution by marginalizing its action.
exercises.solution:
**CTDE (Centralized Training, Decentralized Execution):** the key idea that makes multi-agent learning converge. During **training** you allow each agent's *learning machinery* access to **global information** — all agents' observations and actions. During **execution** each agent acts using **only its own local observation** (its own policy `π_i(a_i | o_i)`), because in deployment agents can't see inside each other. You get the **stability benefits of centralization** for learning and the **realism of decentralization** for acting.

**Why it tames non-stationarity:** the root problem is that, to one agent, the others' changing policies make the environment non-stationary. A **centralized critic** that **conditions on the joint actions/observations of all agents** sees the *full* cause of each outcome — so from the critic's viewpoint the dynamics are **stationary again** (nothing relevant is hidden/moving). It can give each agent a **stable, well-defined value/advantage** even while everyone is learning, breaking the chase-your-tail cycles that independent learners fall into.

**MADDPG (centralized critic, decentralized actors):** each agent `i` has:
- a **decentralized actor** `π_i(a_i | o_i)` — acts on local obs only (used at execution),
- a **centralized critic** `Q_i(x, a_1, ..., a_N)` — during training it sees the **global state and *all* agents' actions**, so its value estimate isn't confounded by others' unobserved choices.
The centralized critic provides a stable gradient to train the local actor. At test time the critics are discarded; agents run their local actors. This is just **actor-critic (Lesson 7) lifted to multi-agent**, with the critic given global info.

**COMA's counterfactual baseline (credit assignment):** in cooperative settings with a **shared team reward**, it's hard to know *which agent* deserves credit — everyone gets the same reward. **COMA (Counterfactual Multi-Agent)** uses a **counterfactual baseline**: it asks *'what would the team reward have been if agent `i` had taken a different (default) action, holding all other agents' actions fixed?'* It computes the centralized critic's value **marginalizing over agent `i`'s action**, and the **advantage = actual `Q` − this counterfactual baseline**. This **isolates agent `i`'s individual contribution** to the team outcome, solving the multi-agent credit-assignment problem (the team-reward analog of the single-agent baseline from Lesson 7). So CTDE + a centralized critic restores stationarity for learning, and counterfactual baselines distribute credit fairly across cooperating agents.

exercises.title: Self-play, league play, and emergent behavior
exercises.prompt: Self-play powered AlphaGo, AlphaStar, and OpenAI Five. Explain why self-play is so powerful for competitive games, the failure modes it creates (cycles, forgetting, overfitting to itself), how league play / opponent pools / fictitious self-play fix them, and the connection to game theory (Nash equilibria) and to LLM self-improvement.
exercises.hint: Self-play = train against copies of yourself → automatic curriculum, opponent scales with you. Problems: strategy cycles (rock-paper-scissors), catastrophic forgetting of old strategies, overfitting to current self. Fixes: maintain a population/league of past+exploiter agents (AlphaStar). Game theory: converge toward Nash/unexploitable. LLMs: self-play debate/critique, RL against verifiers.
exercises.solution:
**Why self-play is so powerful:** in a competitive game you need a stream of appropriately-difficult opponents. **Self-play** — training the agent against **copies of itself** — provides exactly that: an **automatic curriculum** where the opponent is **always at your own skill level** and **scales up as you improve**. You start with random play vs random play and bootstrap to superhuman, with no human opponents or hand-designed curriculum needed. Clean win/loss reward, infinite resets (the game is the simulator), and adversarial pressure that **forces robust strategies**. This is the engine behind AlphaGo/AlphaZero, OpenAI Five (Dota 2), and AlphaStar (StarCraft II).

**Failure modes self-play creates:**
1. **Strategy cycles (non-transitivity):** like rock-paper-scissors, strategy A beats B beats C beats A. Naive self-play can **chase its own tail** — learn a counter to its current self, forget why, and cycle forever without net progress (no stable improvement).
2. **Catastrophic forgetting:** by always training against the **latest** version, the agent **forgets how to beat older strategies**. It becomes strong against its current self but **exploitable by strategies it used to handle** — a real opponent could resurrect an old tactic and win.
3. **Overfitting to itself:** the agent specializes against its **own quirks** rather than learning **generally strong, robust** play, so it transfers poorly to diverse/human opponents.

**Fixes — populations and league play:**
- **Opponent pools / historical checkpoints:** train against a **mix of past versions of yourself**, not just the latest, so you don't forget how to beat old strategies (e.g. sample old checkpoints as opponents).
- **Fictitious self-play:** best-respond to the **average** of past policies (game-theoretically grounded), damping cycles.
- **League play (AlphaStar):** maintain a whole **population/league** of agents with different roles — **main agents** (generalists), **main exploiters** (agents trained specifically to find and punish the main agents' weaknesses), and **league exploiters** (find weaknesses of the whole league). Training against this diverse, adversarial population produces **robust, hard-to-exploit** strategies instead of brittle cyclic ones.

**Connection to game theory:** the goal of these methods is to approach a **Nash equilibrium** — a policy that is **unexploitable** (no opponent can do better by deviating). Cycles and forgetting are symptoms of *not* being at equilibrium; league/fictitious self-play are practical algorithms for **converging toward an (approximate) Nash / unexploitable strategy** in large games, where computing the equilibrium exactly is intractable. The opponent population approximates 'best response to the distribution of strategies', which is the game-theoretic recipe for robustness.

**Connection to LLM self-improvement:** the same idea is reappearing in language models. **Self-play / self-improvement** for LLMs includes **debate and self-critique** (one model argues/critiques another to surface better answers), **generating and solving their own problems**, and **RL against verifiers** (Lesson 12 / GRPO) where the model improves by repeatedly attempting tasks and getting an objective win signal — an automatic curriculum analogous to game self-play, with **mathematical/code verification as the 'win'**. Constitutional AI's self-critique (Lesson 9) is a cooperative cousin. The deep parallel: just as self-play let game agents bootstrap past human skill *without human opponents*, LLM self-play/self-verification aims to let models **improve beyond the ceiling of human-labeled data** — using the model itself (plus a checker) to generate the training signal. The multi-agent lessons — non-stationarity, opponent diversity, avoiding cycles/forgetting, seeking unexploitable strategies — carry directly into this frontier of LLM self-improvement.
